planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil or well-draining potting mix in partial to full shade after frost; Boston fern requires high ambient humidity and consistently moist (never waterlogged) growing conditions — allow the root ball to never dry completely.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Boston fern is the most widely grown ornamental fern in the world — a classic porch, patio, and shade garden plant that produces graceful, arching, bright-green fronds of finely divided pinnate leaflets creating the ultimate soft, feathery, tropical foliage effect. In zones 9–12 it thrives as an outdoor perennial in shaded garden beds, under trees, and in shaded container plantings; throughout the United States it is the defining hanging basket plant for covered porches and shaded outdoor spaces in summer, then overwintered as a houseplant. High humidity is essential — in dry indoor air, foliage yellows and drops its leaflets (pinnules) rapidly, a problem solved by misting, pebble-tray humidifiers, or outdoor placement. 'Bostoniensis' is the classic form; 'Dallas' (tolerates lower humidity), 'Tiger' (heavily ruffled), and 'Florida Ruffle' are popular cultivars. The most widely sold fern in the United States by a considerable margin.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Mount on boards covered with sphagnum moss or plant in hanging baskets in bright indirect light in partial shade; staghorn ferns are epiphytes that root into organic material rather than soil — soak the entire root mass in water for 15–20 minutes weekly, allow to drain fully, and never allow to stand in water. Offsets (pups) can be removed and mounted separately.

directions: Mount on sphagnum-covered boards or plant in hanging baskets in bright indirect light; staghorn fern (elkhorn fern) is one of the most dramatic and sculptural of all ferns — an epiphytic Australian native that produces two distinct frond types: flat, sterile 'shield fronds' that clasp the mounting surface and accumulate organic matter for nutrition, and large, upward-spreading, branched, antler-like 'fertile fronds' that give the plant its extraordinary architectural quality, resembling the antlers of a stag or elk. Plants are mounted on wooden boards, cork slabs, or tree trunks covered with moist sphagnum moss and hung on shaded walls, fences, or tree trunks to create a remarkable living-sculpture effect. Watering consists of soaking the entire root mass weekly (or more frequently in hot weather), then allowing to drain. In zones 9–12 they grow outdoors on trees and fences; elsewhere they are striking houseplants. Offsets (pups) that form around the base can be removed and separately mounted for propagation. Long-lived and can grow to enormous size over decades.

planting.note: Plant in shallow, well-drained hanging baskets or pots allowing the silver-furred rhizomes to drape over the pot edge freely; rabbit's foot fern should be planted at or just above soil level, never burying the decorative rhizomes — the rhizomes creeping over the container edge are a primary ornamental feature of the plant.

directions: Plant in shallow, well-drained baskets allowing the furry rhizomes to drape freely over the edges; rabbit's foot fern is a Fiji native grown as much for its extraordinary rhizomes as for its lacy, dark-green, finely divided fronds — the long, surface-creeping rhizomes are densely covered with silvery-white to light-brown, soft, hair-like scales that have a silky, fur-like texture remarkably similar to a rabbit's foot, draping over the edges of hanging baskets in long, decorative tendrils. The finely dissected, triangular fronds provide a delicate, refined foliage texture above the unusual rhizomes. It is drought-tolerant compared to most ferns, preferring to dry slightly between waterings rather than staying constantly wet. An excellent houseplant and summer shade garden fern that performs well in bright indirect light to partial shade. The fern genus Davallia contains several species with similarly ornamental rhizomes, including D. trichomanoides (squirrel's foot fern) and D. canariensis (hare's foot fern). Easily propagated by cutting and planting rhizome sections.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade after frost; silver lady fern is sensitive to both drought and standing water — maintain even moisture and excellent drainage simultaneously with organic mulch.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; silver lady fern (dwarf tree fern) is a Pacific Island native that is one of the most architecturally impressive of the smaller tropical ferns — it develops a short, stout, fibrous trunk topped by an elegant crown of spreading, once-pinnate, ladder-like fronds with smooth, glossy, dark-green pinnae that give the plant a clean, refined, almost tropical prehistoric quality. As the plant matures it resembles a miniature tree fern, with the arching crown of fronds rising from an increasingly taller fibrous stem, eventually reaching 3–4 feet in height with an elegant, sculptural silhouette distinctly different from the bushier, non-trunked appearance of most ferns. An excellent specimen fern for shaded tropical gardens, container plantings, and indoor bright-shade locations. More tolerant of dry indoor air than many tropical ferns. In zone 9, protect from frost — the crown is the most vulnerable part. 'Silver Lady' is the standard cultivar widely grown.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade after frost; asparagus fern is not a true fern but a member of the Asparagaceae family — its feathery, fern-like appearance comes from small, needle-like modified stems (cladodes) rather than true fronds.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade; asparagus fern (plumosa fern, lace fern) is a South African climbing plant grown for its extraordinarily delicate, feathery, bright-green foliage — the flattened, horizontal branchlets bearing tiny, needle-like cladodes create a texture so fine and airy that it appears almost like mist, making it incomparably useful as a filler and softening element in cut flower arrangements and mixed container plantings. The plant climbs by small, hook-like thorns and in tropical gardens can ascend to 10–15 feet, creating a soft curtain of misty foliage over trellises and fences. In cooler climates it functions as a houseplant or annual container filler. Small white flowers followed by dark berries appear on mature plants. It differs from the more robust A. aethiopicus (emerald fern / Sprengeri fern) in producing a finer, more delicate, horizontal branch structure. A staple of the cut flower industry worldwide for its use as foliage filler in arrangements. Note: berries are mildly toxic.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
